name: Jess Stacy
abstract:
Jess Alexandria Stacy (born Jesse Alexander Stacy; 
August 11, 1904 – January 1, 1995) was an American jazz pianist who gained prominence during the swing era. He may be best remembered for his years with the Benny Goodman band during the late 1930s, particularly his performance at Goodman's 1938 concert at Carnegie Hall.

concerts.label: Benny Goodman and His Swing Orchestra
Newport Jazz Festival: Solo Piano I

works.t: Rosalie: How Long Has This Been Going On?

about_quotes.speaker: James Lincoln Collier
about_quotes.evidence: we were listening to ... Jess Stacy ... These were the heroes.
about_quotes.doc_title: James Lincoln Collier interviewed by Monk Rowe, New York City, New York, January 14, 2007

about_quotes.speaker: Vincent Pelote
about_quotes.evidence: He had Stacy and he had Wilson. ... even though he had a perfectly good pianist in the band, Jess Stacy, who he didn’t use on any of those recordings.
about_quotes.doc_title: Vincent Pelote interviewed by Monk Rowe, Orlando, Florida, February 25, 2017

about_quotes.speaker: Bobby Gordon
about_quotes.evidence: I’d probably have Bobby Hackett or Jack Teagarden and Eddie Condon and Jess Stacy... as my alternate clarinet players whenever I couldn’t make it.
about_quotes.doc_title: Bobby Gordon interviewed by Monk Rowe, Chautauqua, New York, September 12, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Art Hodes
about_quotes.evidence: He's been very kind to me. ... you mentioned earlier that Jess Stacy was always helping you to get jobs back in the old days in Chicago. Right.

about_quotes.speaker: Joe Mares Jr.
about_quotes.evidence: he had George Wetland on drums that he had left in Chicago and Jess Stacy and Pat Patterson that was the band that Paul wanted to put together

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Jones
about_quotes.evidence: We used to have block association meetings, you know, so we were both talking about Benny. So Jess would say, "That son of a gun is crazy."
